title: Les courses et la nourriture
description: Food shopping: partitive articles (some), quantities, and polite shopping phrases.

cards.notes: Masculine. With *some*: **du pain**. *Je voudrais du pain* — I'd like some bread.
Masculine. With *some*: **du lait**. Often bought *une bouteille de lait* (a bottle of milk).
Plural. With *some*: **des fruits**. French treats fruit as countable: *des fruits*.
Plural. With *some*: **des légumes**. *J'achète des légumes au marché.*
Feminine. With *some*: **de la viande**. *Je mange de la viande* — I eat (some) meat.
Masculine. With *some*: **du fromage**. *beaucoup de fromage* — a lot of cheese.
Feminine, but starts with a vowel. With *some*: **de l'eau** (not *de la eau*). *Je voudrais de l'eau.*
Masculine. With *some*: **du café**. *un café* can also mean a cup of coffee at a café.
*au marché* — at the market. Outdoor market for fresh produce. *J'achète des fruits au marché.*
*au supermarché* — at the supermarket. *Faire les courses* — to do the (grocery) shopping.
Polite way to ask for something — softer than *Je veux* (I want). *Je voudrais du pain, s'il vous plaît.*
Essential shopping question. Note the space before **?** — standard in French. Also: *Ça coûte combien ?*
Quantity expressions take **de** only (never *du/de la/des*): *un kilo de pommes* — a kilo of apples.
Another quantity: *une bouteille de lait* — a bottle of milk. Always **de**, no article after.

steps.title: Saying "some" — the partitive
steps.body:
# Du, de la, de l', des

In English you can say *I'd like bread* or *I'd like some bread* — the *some* is optional. In French it is **not** optional. When you mean an unspecified amount of something, you must use a **partitive article**, which all translate as *some* / *any*.

Which one depends on the noun:

| Noun type | Partitive | Example |
|---|---|---|
| masculine | **du** | *du pain* — some bread |
| feminine | **de la** | *de la viande* — some meat |
| starts with a vowel | **de l'** | *de l'eau* — some water |
| plural | **des** | *des légumes* — some vegetables |

- *Je voudrais **du** café.* — I'd like some coffee. (masc.)
- *Tu manges **de la** viande ?* — Do you eat (any) meat? (fem.)
- *Il y a **de l'**eau.* — There is some water. (vowel)
- *J'achète **des** fruits.* — I'm buying some fruit. (plural)

Think of it as **du = de + le**, **des = de + les**. The form follows the noun's gender and number.

exercise.prompt: Match each French food word with its English meaning.
Match each partitive form with when you use it.

exercise.hint: *Eau* is feminine but starts with a vowel, so *de la* shortens to *de l'*.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I would like some bread.* (Use the polite form.)

steps.title: Quantities take "de" only
steps.body:
## A kilo of, a bottle of, a lot of

When you give a **precise quantity**, the partitive (*du / de la / des*) disappears. You use plain **de** (or **d'** before a vowel) with no article:

| Expression | Example |
|---|---|
| un kilo **de** | *un kilo **de** pommes* — a kilo of apples |
| une bouteille **de** | *une bouteille **de** lait* — a bottle of milk |
| beaucoup **de** | *beaucoup **de** fromage* — a lot of cheese |
| un peu **de** | *un peu **d'**eau* — a little water |

Compare:

- *Je voudrais **du** lait.* — I'd like some milk. (unspecified)
- *Je voudrais **une bouteille de** lait.* — I'd like a bottle of milk. (quantified)

> Rule of thumb: any time there's a measure word (kilo, bottle, lot, little, glass...), it's followed by **de** alone — never *du*, *de la*, or *des*.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *I would like a bottle of milk.*
exercise.hint: Polite phrase + quantity (*une bouteille de*) + the noun. No article after *de*.

exercise.prompt: Arrange to mean: *a kilo of apples at the market.*
exercise.hint: Quantity (*un kilo de*) + *pommes*, then *au marché* (at the market).

steps.title: Polite shopping phrases
steps.body:
## At the shop

A few phrases make shopping in French go smoothly:

- **Je voudrais...** — *I would like...* The polite request. Much softer than *Je veux* (I want), which can sound blunt.
- **C'est combien ?** — *How much is it?* Note the space before the question mark — French always puts one there.
- **s'il vous plaît** — *please.* Tack it onto any request: *Je voudrais du fromage, s'il vous plaît.*

Where you shop:

- **au marché** — at the (open-air) market, for fresh produce.
- **au supermarché** — at the supermarket.
- **faire les courses** — to do the grocery shopping.

A tiny dialogue:

> — *Bonjour ! Je voudrais un kilo de pommes, s'il vous plaît.*
> — *Voilà. Autre chose ?*
> — *C'est combien ?*

exercise.prompt: How do you ask *How much is it?* in French?
exercise.hint: *C'est* (it is) + *combien* (how much). Remember the space before the question mark.
*Légumes* is plural, so the partitive is *des*.

exercise.prompt: Translate: *I buy some meat at the supermarket.*
accept: J'achète de la viande au supermarché.
j'achète de la viande au supermarché.
J'achete de la viande au supermarche.
distractors: Je achète de la viande au supermarché.
